coredata.dc:title: S, N co-doped carbon dots induced effective chlorine ion repulsion in TiO2 nanorod clusters for photoelectrochemical seawater splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Ziming
He, Qiuchen
Qv, Yang
Zhou, Feng
Zhan, Su
coredata.dc:description: It is essential to promote seawater photoelectrolysis technologies for reducing the dependency on fossil fuels and alleviating the freshwater resources shortage. However, the undesirable chloride oxidation reaction (CER) on the anode side has seriously limited their application. Here, S, N co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) were prepared and successfully decorated on the surface of TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanode via hydrothermal process to inhibit the CER in photoelectrochemical (PEC) seawater splitting. The prepared S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanode exhibited a photocurrent density of 34.4 μA cm−1 (at 1.23 V RHE), which was around 3 times for that of pure TiO2. More importantly, it exhibited a lower ClO− yield. After 1 h of continuous operation, the concentration of the ClO− was only 6 μM in the final reaction liquid, which was nearly half of that for pure TiO2. It was suggested that S, N-CDs could be potential ideal electrode materials in PEC seawater splitting systems, due to effectively chlorine-repulsing, improving photoelectric properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar hydrogen production
Photoelectrochemical
Seawater splitting
Carbon dots
Chlorine repulsion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.52271340, 51879018).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–b) SEM images, (c) TEM image and (d) HTEM image of TiO2 nanorod clusters. (e) TEM image, HTEM image and size distribution of S, N-CDs. (f) HTEM image of S, N-CDs/TiO2. (g) EDS elemental mapping of S, N-CDs/TiO2.
(a) XRD pattern (c) FT-IR spectrum of S, N-CDs. (b) XRD patterns (d) FT-IR spectra of TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 nanorod clusters.
High-resolution Ti2p spectra of (a) TiO2 and (b) S, N-CDs/TiO2. High-resolution O1s spectra of (c) TiO2 and (d) S, N-CDs/TiO2. High-resolution (e) N1s and (f) S2p spectra of S, N-CDs/TiO2.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of S, N-CDs. (b) UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanode and S, N-CDs/TiO2. (c) The corresponding Tauc plots of TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2.

(a) Photocurrent density at 1.23 V vs. RHE. and the concentrations of productive ClO− after continuous operating 1h of S, N-CDs/TiO2 with various dosages of S, N-CDs. (b) LSV spectra, (c) transient photocurrent density responses and (d) EIS Nyquist plots of TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2.
M − S plots of (a) TiO2 and (b) S, N-CDs/TiO2.
(a) The zeta potential of S, N-CDs. (b) Schematic mechanism of the PEC water oxidation and chlorine-repulsive performance of S, N-CDs/TiO2.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: “Chlorine evolution reaction” was abbreviated to “CER”. “S, N co-doped carbon dots” was abbreviated to “S, N-CDs”. “photoelectrochemical” was abbreviated to “PEC”. “N-doped carbon dots” was abbreviated to “N-CDs”. “Hydrogen evolution reaction” was abbreviated to “HER”. “Oxygen evolution reaction” was abbreviated to “OER”. “Carbon paper” was abbreviated to “CP”. “Simulated seawater” was abbreviated to “SSW”.

head.ce:title: S, N co-doped carbon dots induced effective chlorine ion repulsion in TiO2 nanorod clusters for photoelectrochemical seawater splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ship-Machinery Maintenance and Manufacture for Ministry of Transport, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ship-Machinery Maintenance and Manufacture for Ministry of Transport

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Ship-Machinery Maintenance and Manufacture for Ministry of Transport, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116026, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is essential to promote seawater photoelectrolysis technologies for reducing the dependency on fossil fuels and alleviating the freshwater resources shortage. However, the undesirable chloride oxidation reaction (CER) on the anode side has seriously limited their application. Here, S, N co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) were prepared and successfully decorated on the surface of TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanode via hydrothermal process to inhibit the CER in photoelectrochemical (PEC) seawater splitting. The prepared S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanode exhibited a photocurrent density of 34.4 μA cm−1 (at 1.23 V RHE), which was around 3 times for that of pure TiO2. More importantly, it exhibited a lower ClO− yield. After 1 h of continuous operation, the concentration of the ClO− was only 6 μM in the final reaction liquid, which was nearly half of that for pure TiO2. It was suggested that S, N-CDs could be potential ideal electrode materials in PEC seawater splitting systems, due to effectively chlorine-repulsing, improving photoelectric properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing renewable and green energy to address the global energy crisis is significant but challenging [1]. Solar hydrogen production, storing renewable solar energy by driving hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), is an attractive solution to the problem faced by sustainable energy conversion [2–6]. PEC water splitting provides a viable strategy for solar-to-hydrogen conversion, as one of the most promising technologies [7–11]. In this process, a photoelectrode with semiconductor photocatalysts absorbs solar energy, holes and electronics will be generated and transferred to the surface of anodes and cathodes through the external circuit, respectively. Then the oxidation in anodic and reduction in cathodic will occur simultaneously. Water will be consumed as the only reactant in the reaction [12,13]. However, the issue is that water shortages may worsen if vast amounts of freshwater are used for future hydrogen fuel formation [14–18]. Seawater is the most abundant natural resource available on earth. Roundly 97% of the water resources are stored in the ocean [19]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop seawater as the aqueous electrolyte feedstock in PEC water splitting cells. But it still presents much challenges for implementation in practical application [20].

The first major challenge is the evolution of undesirable CER. Different from fresh water, seawater contains large amounts of chloride ions with a concentration of around 0.5 M [21]. According to the reaction thermodynamics and kinetics, oxygen evolution reaction (OER, 1.23 V vs SHE) and CER (1.36 V vs SHE) have close thermodynamic potentials. However, OER is a four-electron oxidation, while CER, as a two-electron oxidation, has a clear kinetic advantage [16,22–28]. CER is favorable in the competition with a lower overpotential compared with OER. Thus, Cl− will be preferentially oxidized to over water molecules oxidation in the anodic reaction, generating oxidation products such as ClO− and Cl2. Nevertheless, it is undesirable that reactive chlorine species as by-products of novel sustainable energy applications. Although they are important products in water treatment and harmful microbiological control, there would quickly exceed demand if generated in the future hydrogen industry [29]. It will be a huge threat to the environment and ecology. In 2019, Kuang et al. [30] constructed a polyanion sulfate/carbonate anionic layer in NiFe/NiSx-Ni foam anode to repel Cl− anions efficiently. They demonstrated that the incorporation of negatively charged in photoanode is a feasible solution to inhibit the CER but the involved research is seldom reported after that. Moreover, the cost is one of the critical points in practical application. It should be paid more attention to finding an economically appealing substitute. As a novel carbon-based nanomaterial with sizes below 10 nm, carbon dots are ideal support of negatively charged functional groups, which can effectively repel anions such as SO4 2−, Cl− and NO3 − [31–34]. Additionally, with low cost, environmentally friendly, a wide range of raw materials and their unique photoelectric properties, there have been widely reported in recent years to be applied in electrocatalysis and photocatalysis [35–41]. Therefore, carbon dots have potential to be used as chlorine-repulsive module for PEC water splitting system.

Herein, this work developed a synthetic strategy to prepare S, N-CDs modified rutile TiO2 nanorod clusters (S, N-CDs/TiO2) for PEC seawater oxidation reaction. The S, N-CDs have abundant negatively charged functional groups, repelling Cl− anions hence inhibiting the evolution of reactive chlorine species in seawater on TiO2-based photoanode. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report upon the chlorine ion repulsion properties of carbon dots in PEC seawater splitting. Combined with photoanode characterization, a detailed mechanism was presented here for the Cl− anions repelling process. This work has demonstrated that S, N-CDs exhibit efficient performance as a chlorine-repulsive module in photoelectrolysis and it provides an alternative way to design the photoanode in PEC cells for seawater splitting.

Titanium butoxide (TBOT), thiourea, sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) and citric acid were purchased from Macklin chemistry reagent company. Sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium sulfate were provided by China reagent net. All the reagents employed in this study were analytical grade and did not require further purification. The carbon paper (CP) was provided by Toray Industries Co., Ltd.

S, N-CDs were synthesized via the microwave method. Typically, 1 g of citric acid and 3 g of thiourea were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The formed transparent solution was moved into a 600 W microwave oven and heated for 10 min. The obtained dark brown solid products were S, N-CDs crude product and dissolved in water. Afterward, the suspension was filtrated through a 200 nm filter to remove agglomerated particles. S, N-CDs solution was final obtained after further purification using a dialysis membrane (cut off of 100–500 Da) for 24 h. At last, the resulting S, N-CDs powder was collected by drying the purified solution and kept for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 nanorod clusters were prepared on CP substrates using a simple hydrothermal process. 600 μL TBOT was added to 20 mL HCl (2 M) in an ice-water bath. Next, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, the CP (2 × 3 cm2) was put into it with an incline angle against the wall of the autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained TiO2 nanoclusters photoanodes was dried at 60 °C and annealed at 200 °C in the air for 3 h.

The TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanodes were immersed in 20 mL S, N-CDs solution with different concentrations (0 mg L−1, 1.25 mg L−1, 2.5 mg L−1, 3.75 mg L−1, 5.0 mg L−1 and 6.25 mg L−1) and heated at 120 °C for 4 h to form the S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanodes. Finally, the obtained products were washed thoroughly and dried at 60 °C.

The morphologies of the samples were characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Supra 55 Sapphire) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 F). The crystalline structures of the samples were measured by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD, Rigaku RINT-2000) with Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. The elementary composition and the chemical structure of the samples were observed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB250Xi) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer Frontier I spectrophotometer). The UV–vis absorption spectra and UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were obtained on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (VARIAN, UV1006M031). Zeta potentials were measured by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90.

The PEC measurement was carried out using a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (Crrotest CS300 M). The counter electrode was a platinum foil, while the reference electrode was a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode. The product photoanode was used as the working electrode. The electrolyte is simulated seawater (SSW, 0.5 M NaCl) and aqueous solution containing 0.10 M Na2SO4. A 300 W xenon lamp acted as light source. The measured potentials versus Ag/AgCl electrode can be converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) by the Nernst equation:
(1)
E RHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.0591 × pH + 0.1976

SEM and TEM were used to investigate the morphology of the samples. The SEM and TEM images of the TiO2 sample revealed that the TiO2 exhibited cluster architecture with a width of about 3 μm, composed of nanorods and grown on CP uniformly (Fig. 1 a–c). Viewed from the HTEM image, the clear lattice fringes of 0.32 nm could be found, corresponding to the (110) planes of rutile TiO2 (Fig. 1d) [42–44]. The TEM image of S, N-CDs was shown in Fig. 1e and the inset was the related HTEM image. It was found that the prepared S, N-CDs had a spherical morphology and their diameters were distributed between 2.6 and 5.0 nm with an average diameter of about 4.98 nm. While, the HTEM showed the lattice fringes of 0.23 nm for S, N-CDs, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite [45,46]. Figs. S1a–c were the SEM and TEM images for S, N-CDs/TiO2 sample. It could be seen that there was no obvious change after modifying with S, N-CDs. But, from the HTEM images as shown in Fig. 1f, the surface of the S, N-CDs/TiO2 sample presented clearly rougher than that of the TiO2 sample and both the lattice fringes for TiO2 and S, N-CDs could be found. In addition, EDS element mapping of samples was shown in Fig. 1g and Fig. S2. The signals of elements Ti, O, C, N and S could be clearly detected for S, N-CDs/TiO2 while without any N and S signals for the TiO2 sample. These results confirmed that the S, N-CDs were successfully decorated on the surface of TiO2 nanorod clusters.

The crystal structures of the S, N-CDs, TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples were further studied by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the XRD pattern of the S, N-CDs displayed a broad peak centered at around 26.0°, corresponding to the graphitized carbon peak [47,48]. In Fig. 2b, the XRD patterns of the TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples were well indexed to rutile TiO2 (PDF 88–1172). The sharp diffraction peak appeared at about 25.0°, belonging to the CP (Fig. S3). No typical signal of S, N-CDs was observed for S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples, which might be attributed to the low content [46].

The surface functional groups of the samples were analyzed by FT-IR techniques. As shown in Fig. 2c, the FT-IR spectrum of S, N-CDs showed two broad absorption peaks around 3430 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1, which belonged to stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H and C–H, respectively [49]. Two strong peaks at 2060 and 1711 cm−1 were corresponding to –CNS and CO, respectively [50]. The peaks at 1404, 1189 and 1080 cm−1 were attributed to C–N, C–S/C–O and SO3, respectively [47,50,51]. These results indicated that the S, N-CDs were abundant in oxygenous, nitrogenous and sulfurous functional groups on their surface. As shown in Fig. 2d, the peak at 550 cm−1 was displayed in both TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–Ti–O bonds [52]. From the spectrum of S, N-CDs/TiO2 sample, two distinctive broad peaks centered around 3600 and 3250 cm−1, which were possibly originated from the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H and C–H, could be observed, informing the successfully loading of S, N-CDs on the TiO2 nanorod clusters.

The XPS spectra were used to analyze the chemical structure of the samples. As shown in Fig. S4a, the S, N-CDs exhibited five characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.96 eV, 531.84 eV, 227 eV and 163.3 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, S2p and S2s, respectively [50,53]. The elemental composition of S, N-CDs were revealed to be C 66.23%, N 12.49%, O 19.52% and S 1.77% (Table S1). The high resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. S4b) showed three peaks of C–C/CC at 284.6 eV, C–N/C–O/C–S at 285.3 eV and CO at 288.2 eV [53]. In the high resolution XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. S4c), three peaks at 399.4 eV, 399.9 eV and 400.4 eV were attributed to graphitic N, pyrrolic N and amino N, respectively [45]. The high resolution XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. S4d) exhibited three types of peaks including CO, C–O and C–OH at 531.4 eV, 531.9 eV and 532.7 eV, respectively [54]. In the high resolution XPS spectra of S2p (Fig. S4e) the peaks at 161.8 eV, 162.4 eV and 163.2 eV, 164.4 eV were corresponded to alkyl S2p3/2, S2p1/2 and thiophenic S2p3/2, S2p1/2, respectively. These results further verified the presence of oxygenous, amine and sulfonic groups on the surface of S, N-CDs [50]. The XPS survey spectrum of pure TiO2 photoanode sample was consistent with recent reports and the Ti2p and O1s were obtained in both TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples (Fig. S4a). As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the peaks at 459.7/465.5 eV and 458.7/464.4 eV were observed in the Ti2p high-resolution spectra of the TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples, which were attributed to the pair of Ti 2p3/2, Ti 2p1/2, respectively [55]. The peaks at 531/532.7 eV and 529.9/531.7 eV could be clearly observed in the O1s high-resolution spectra of the TiO2 (Fig. 3c) and S, N-CDs/TiO2 (Fig. 3d) samples, corresponding to Ti–O and Ti–OH/C–O groups, respectively [56]. It should be noted that the peaks of the S, N-CDs/TiO2 sample was positively shifted compared with pure TiO2 which can be explained by the lone pair of electrons on Ti atoms to shift to C atoms of the S, N-CDs causing the reducing of the electron density on Ti atoms [57]. Additionally, all peaks of O1s were negatively shifted after incorporating S, N-CDs on TiO2 surface, which could be owing to the combination of C atoms in S, N-CDs and O atoms in TiO2. This result indicated that in S, N-CDs/TiO2 sample, the S, N-CDs were decorated on the surface of TiO2 by chemical bonds [58]. Additionally, compared with that of TiO2 sample, the survey S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanode sample showed two slightly weaker peaks of S2p and N1s at 164.2 eV and 400 eV. In Fig. 3e and f, the high resolution N1s spectra showed three peaks at 400.2 eV (graphitic N), 400.9 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.6 eV (amino N), the high resolution S2p spectra of S, N-CDs/TiO2 presented two peaks at 164.1 eV (S 2p3/2) and 165.9 eV (S 2p1/2). These results indicated that N, S-CDs were successful loaded to the surface of TiO2 nanorods clusters which well agreed with the results of FT-IR, TEM and SEM.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of S, N-CDs and UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the pristine TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanode and S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanode were collected to probe the light harvesting capability. As shown in Fig. 4 a, two absorption peaks at 270 nm and 340 nm were found in the spectrum of S, N-CDs, which assigned to CC bonds (π = π∗ transition) and CO bonds (n = π∗ transition), respectively [59–61]. As shown in Fig. 4b, although the visible light harvesting of TiO2 was slight improved after coupling with S, N-CDs, the absorption edge showed no clear shift, which was around 410 nm. It was presumably due to the S, N-CDs only presented on the surface of TiO2. The band gap of the samples could be calculated by the following formula [44]:
(2)
ahv =
A
( hv −
E g
)
n ∕ 2
where α, h and υ are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant and light frequency, respectively. Where Eg, A and n are the band-gap, proportionality constant and the characteristic integer, respectively. TiO2 was a direct band gap semiconductor. Thus, the value of n was 1 in this experiment. According to Fig. 4c, the band-gap energies of the pristine TiO2 photoanodes was 2.8 eV and the band-gap energies of the S, N-CDs/TiO2 was around 2.7 eV, which only narrowed 0.1 eV [62].

The PEC seawater splitting performances for the samples were evaluated in SSW. The line graph in Fig. 5 a showed the photocurrent density (1.23 V vs. RHE) with different concentrations of S, N-CDs added during preparation. It was found that the photocurrent density of the S, N-CDs/TiO2 improved with the concentrations of S, N-CDs from 0 to 3.75 mg L−1 but it decreased with the concentrations of S, N-CDs from 3.75 mg L−1 to 6.25 mg L−1. The S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanodes exhibited a photocurrent density of 34.4 μA cm−1 (1.23V RHE) as the concentrations of the prepared dispersion for S, N-CDs is 3.75 mg L−1, which was three times for pristine TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanode. This value was comparable to those values from other TiO2-based photoanode (Table S2) [42,63–71]. Moreover, the linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) curves (Fig. 5b) and transient photocurrent responses (Fig. 5c) were further certified that after modified with S, N-CDs, the photocurrent density of S, N-CDs/TiO2 remarkably increased at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The improved photocurrent might arise from the presence of the S, N-CDs on their surface contributing to the excellent electrical conductivity of the semiconductor [63,72]. EIS measurements were carried out, as shown in Fig. 5d. It was clearly shown that the arc radius of S, N-CDs/TiO2 sample was smaller compared to TiO2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. This result indicated that the S, N-CDs/TiO2 had a smaller resistance value and higher separation efficiency of photo-induced electrons/holes pairs compared to TiO2, which could be ascribed to the electronic interaction between S, N-CDs and TiO2 nanorod clusters. As shown in Fig. S5, all TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 showed acceptable long-operation stability, tested through 10-h continuous operation.

The formed rate of ClO− in the anode was determined by a thionine test [73] (Fig. 5a, histogram). The UV–vis absorption spectra of thionine solution with different concentrations of ClO− were shown in Fig. S6a and the standard curve between the absorbance at 600 nm and the ClO− concentration were shown in Fig. S6b. It could be seen that the formed rate of ClO− obviously decreased with the increase of the amount of the S, N-CDs. The concentrations of productive ClO− was 11.4 μM with pristine TiO2 nanorod clusters photoanode after 1 h of continuous operation but only 6 μM with S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanode. These results revealed that the introduce of the S, N-CDs improved the PEC water oxidation ability of photoanode, meanwhile effectively inhibiting the formation of the ClO− in photoanode.

The Mott-Schottky (M − S) measurements were conducted and the M − S plots of the photoanode samples (at 10 kHz and 5 kHz) were demonstrated in Fig. 6 a and b, respectively. As seen that both the M − S plots of the pristine TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples exhibited a positive slope, as expected for n-type semiconductor [52]. Therefore, the flat band potential was consistent with the bottom of the conduction band (CB) for TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 samples [60]. According to the extrapolation of the M − S plots to the x-intercept, the bottom of CB for TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 were −0.01 eV and 0.09 eV, respectively. The band-gap energies of the pristine TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 were 2.8 eV and 2.7 eV, respectively. Thus, the top of valence band (VB) for TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 were both calculated to be around 2.79 eV. It indicated that after decorated S, N-CDs, the CB was lowed and the band-gap energy was narrowed but the VB had no change compared with TiO2. However, the narrow of the band-gap energies was not bringing the improvement in the PEC performance under visible light (Fig. S7). The higher photocurrent density of the S, N-CDs/TiO2 was not attributed to the photosensitized, while possibly from the excellent electrical conductivity of the S, N-CDs. The carrier density (Nd) was estimated by the following equation:
(3)
1
C 2
=
2
ε
ε 0
e
N d
[ V −
V
FB
−
kT
e 0
]
where ε is the dielectric constant of the anode material, ε 0 = 8.86 × 10−12 F m−1, e = 1.6 × 10−19. According to past reports, smaller MS slope corresponded to larger carrier density. Thus, it is obtained that the carrier density of S, N-CDs/TiO2 were larger than pure TiO2, which could be attributed to the contribution of the S, N-CDs on electron transport [21,23]. Zeta potential of the S, N-CDs was −10.7 mV and the zeta distribution data was shown in Fig. 7 a. Additionally, the negative potentials of S, N-CDs could be attributed to the rich negative functional groups, which were close to the results of FT-IR and XPS measurements [33]. The results of Zeta potential tests for TiO2 and S, N-CDs/TiO2 were found in Fig. S8. It was observed that the surface charges of the samples were changed from 5.02 to −18.5 mV after modified S, N-CDs, indicated that the S, N-CDs can clearly change the surface charges of the catalysts and form a chlorine-repulsive barrier.

Based on the findings, a potential mechanism for the effective inhibitory CER over S, N-CDs/TiO2 in PEC seawater splitting has been proposed. As shown in Fig. 7b, for the TiO2 sample, under UV light irradiation, the photon-generated electrons were excited from VB to its CB and transferred to the cathode through the external circuit, leaving the hole in the VB of TiO2. Then, the holes were preferentially consumed by the process of CER due to the dynamic advantage compared to the OER. After modification with S, N-CDs, the VB of S, N-CDs/TiO2 was not changed so that OER would still proceed. The photoelectron was moved faster to the surface of the photoanode and further delivered to the cathode through an external circuit due to the excellent electroconductibility of S, N-CDs, making an extra electronic channel. Furthermore, the electronegative S, N-CDs constructed a chlorine-repulsive barrier which effectively prevent the contact of Cl− with the catalytic site, ultimately leading to higher photocurrent density in the S, N-CDs/TiO2 anode, generating a faster OER reaction rate while suppressing the CER.

In summary, the S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanodes were successfully prepared and demonstrated by a multi-step hydrothermal method. The S, N-CDs/TiO2 photoanodes not only exhibited a good water oxidation ability for PEC water splitting, but also displayed outstanding anti-CER performance. This was mainly because of that the S, N-CDs served as the electronic/holes transport channel and repelled chloride ion cells, which dramatically improved the electroconductibility of TiO2 nanorod clusters while inducing effective chlorine ion repulsions. More importantly, our result clearly indicated that the chlorine-repulsive properties of S, N-CDs could be attributed to the negative charge on their surface, which originated from their abundant superficial functional groups. This demonstration provides a new idea for effectively inhibiting adverse CER in seawater splitting system and gives an alternative avenue for designing next-generation sustainable PEC electrode materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.52271340, 51879018).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2023.09.203.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in energy conversion applications of carbon dots: from optoelectronic devices to electrocatalysis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Particulate photocatalysts for light-driven water splitting: mechanisms, challenges, and design strategies
